sections.title: What Are Counters?
sections.explanation: Korean uses specific counting words called counters (단위 명사 or 분류사) between a number and a noun. You cannot simply put a number next to a noun as in English. For example, you cannot say '세 사과' for 'three apples' — you must say 사과 세 개, inserting the counter 개 after the number. Every category of noun has its own counter: people, animals, objects, drinks, and more each require a different counter word. This is similar to how English sometimes says 'two sheets of paper' or 'three heads of cattle,' except Korean does it for nearly everything.
sections.tip: Think of counters as the Korean equivalent of 'cups of,' 'pieces of,' or 'bottles of' — except they are required for all counting, not just special cases.

exercises.prompt: Why can't you say '세 사과' for 'three apples' in Korean?
options: You need a counter between the number and noun
사과 is not a countable noun
exercises.explanation: Korean requires a counter word when counting. The correct form is 사과 세 개, with the counter 개 after the number.

exercises.prompt: What is a counter (단위 명사) in Korean?
options: A word that replaces the noun
A specific counting word used between a number and a noun
exercises.explanation: Counters are special words that classify what is being counted. They appear after the number: noun + number + counter.

sections.title: Counters with Native Korean Numbers
sections.explanation: Most everyday counters use the native Korean number system (하나, 둘, 셋...). The most important ones are: 명 (people, neutral), 분 (people, polite/honorific), 마리 (animals), 개 (general objects), 살 (age), 잔 (cups/glasses of drinks), 병 (bottles), 그루 (trees). Examples: 친구 세 명 (three friends), 강아지 두 마리 (two puppies), 연필 다섯 개 (five pencils), 스무 살 (20 years old), 커피 한 잔 (one cup of coffee), 맥주 두 병 (two bottles of beer), 나무 세 그루 (three trees). Remember to use the shortened number forms before counters: 한, 두, 세, 네, 스무.
sections.tip: Start by memorizing these seven counters — they cover the vast majority of daily counting: 명/분 (people), 마리 (animals), 개 (things), 살 (age), 잔 (cups), 병 (bottles), 그루 (trees).

examples.en: One cup of coffee, please. (잔 = cup counter)
There are three trees. (그루 = tree counter)
two bottles of beer (병 = bottle counter)

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'three cups of tea'?
exercises.explanation: 셋 shortens to 세 before a counter. Tea (차) + 세 (3) + 잔 (cup counter) = 차 세 잔. Sino-Korean 삼 is not used with 잔.

exercises.prompt: Which counter is used for animals?
exercises.explanation: 마리 is the counter for animals: 고양이 한 마리 (one cat), 새 다섯 마리 (five birds). 명 is for people and 개 is for general objects.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'two bottles of water'?
exercises.explanation: 둘 shortens to 두 before a counter. Water (물) + 두 (2) + 병 (bottle counter) = 물 두 병.

exercises.prompt: Which counter do you use for trees?
exercises.explanation: 그루 is the specific counter for trees: 나무 한 그루 (one tree). 개 is for general objects and 마리 is for animals.

exercises.prompt: How do you say 'She is 20 years old'?
exercises.explanation: Age uses the native Korean number system with the counter 살. 스물 shortens to 스무 before 살, so 20 years old = 스무 살.

sections.title: Counters with Sino-Korean Numbers
sections.explanation: Some counters specifically use the Sino-Korean number system (일, 이, 삼...). The most common are: 원 (won, Korean currency), 번 (times or ordinal numbers), 층 (floors of a building), 년 (years), 월 (months of the year), 일 (days of the month), 분 (minutes). Examples: 천 원 (1,000 won), 삼 번 (number 3 / three times), 오 층 (5th floor), 이천이십오 년 (the year 2025), 삼월 (March), 십오 일 (the 15th day), 삼십 분 (30 minutes). These counters never use native Korean numbers — saying 하나 원 or 둘 층 would be incorrect.
sections.tip: A helpful rule: if it involves formal measurements, money, dates, or floor numbers, use Sino-Korean numbers. If it involves counting physical things you can see, use native Korean numbers.

examples.en: It is on the 3rd floor. (층 = floor counter)

exercises.explanation: Currency (원) uses Sino-Korean numbers. 7,000 = 칠 (7) + 천 (thousand) = 칠천 원. Native Korean 일곱 is never used with 원.
Building floors (층) use Sino-Korean numbers: 십 (10) + 층 = 십 층. Native Korean 열 is not used with 층.

exercises.prompt: Which number system is used with 번 (times/number)?
exercises.explanation: 번 uses Sino-Korean numbers: 일 번 (number 1), 이 번 (number 2 / two times), 삼 번 (number 3 / three times).
Months and days use Sino-Korean numbers. June is 유월 (irregular form of 육월) and the 5th is 오일. So June 5th = 유월 오일.

sections.title: Word Order with Counters
sections.explanation: The standard word order for counting in Korean is: Noun + Number + Counter. For example: 사과 세 개 (apple three-piece = three apples). However, there is an alternative pattern using the possessive particle 의: Number + Counter + 의 + Noun. For example: 세 개의 사과 (three-piece's apple = three apples). The first pattern (noun + number + counter) is more common in spoken Korean and everyday conversation. The second pattern (number + counter + 의 + noun) is more common in written Korean and formal contexts. Both are grammatically correct. In casual speech, you can also drop 의: 세 개 사과.
sections.tip: For everyday speaking, use: Noun + Number + Counter (사과 세 개). For writing or formal speech, you may see: Number + Counter + 의 + Noun (세 개의 사과).

exercises.prompt: What is the standard spoken word order for 'five pencils'?
exercises.explanation: The standard spoken pattern is Noun + Number + Counter: 연필 (pencil) + 다섯 (5) + 개 (counter) = 연필 다섯 개.

exercises.prompt: Which sentence uses the formal/written pattern?
exercises.explanation: The formal/written pattern is Number + Counter + 의 + Noun: 두 마리의 고양이 (two cats).

sections.title: Irregular Number Forms Before Counters
sections.explanation: Five native Korean numbers change their form when they appear directly before a counter. These shortened forms are mandatory — using the full form before a counter sounds unnatural. The changes are: 하나 → 한 (one), 둘 → 두 (two), 셋 → 세 (three), 넷 → 네 (four), 스물 → 스무 (twenty). Examples: 한 개 (one thing, not 하나 개), 두 명 (two people, not 둘 명), 세 마리 (three animals, not 셋 마리), 네 잔 (four cups, not 넷 잔), 스무 살 (twenty years old, not 스물 살). Numbers from 다섯 (5) onward do not change: 다섯 개, 여섯 명, 일곱 마리, etc. Compound numbers also follow this rule: 스물한 개 = twenty-one things (스물 stays full when not directly before the counter, 하나 becomes 한 because it is directly before 개).
sections.tip: Only five numbers shorten: 하나→한, 둘→두, 셋→세, 넷→네, 스물→스무. All other native Korean numbers keep their full form before counters.

examples.en: Just one more cup, please. (하나 → 한 before 잔)
I bought four bottles. (넷 → 네 before 병)
I am 20 years old. (스물 → 스무 before 살)

exercises.prompt: What is the correct form: ___ 개 (one thing)?
exercises.explanation: 하나 shortens to 한 before a counter: 한 개 (one thing). Using 하나 개 or 일 개 is incorrect.
넷 shortens to 네 before a counter: 네 마리 (four animals). The full form 넷 마리 is incorrect.

exercises.prompt: Which number does NOT change before a counter?
exercises.explanation: 다섯 (5) keeps its full form before counters: 다섯 개, 다섯 명. Only 하나, 둘, 셋, 넷, and 스물 have shortened forms.
